era_context: Early 4th century BCE, a period of transition in the Greek world. The Corinthian War has ended (387 BCE), and the King's Peace has established a nominal framework for Greek interstate relations, but this framework is breaking down. Sparta remains the dominant military power but faces increasing challenges. Thebes is beginning its rise to prominence. Philosophically, Plato is active in Athens. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II is stable but faces periodic revolts.

events.title: Spartan occupation of Thebes' Cadmea fortress
events.description: Spartan forces, under the command of Phoebidas, occupy the Cadmea, the acropolis of Thebes, under the pretext of supporting the oligarchic faction in the city. This act of aggression violates the King's Peace and marks a turning point in Greek history. The occupation galvanizes Theban resistance and ultimately leads to the liberation of Thebes in 379 BCE and the rise of Epaminondas. The Cadmea occupation is a critical catalyst for Theban independence and the subsequent Theban hegemony. It demonstrates Spartan willingness to use military force to maintain control, but also provokes the very resistance that will undermine Spartan power.

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica, Book XV

events.certainty_note: This event is well-attested in contemporary and near-contemporary sources. Xenophon provides a detailed account. The date of 382 BCE is widely accepted in modern scholarship.

events.title: Theban resistance and underground movement begins
events.description: Following the Spartan occupation of the Cadmea, Theban democrats and patriots begin organizing resistance to Spartan rule. Pelopidas and other leaders of the democratic faction go into exile or underground. This period marks the beginning of the movement that will lead to the liberation of Thebes in 379 BCE. The resistance movement is initially small and clandestine but grows in strength and organization. This underground movement becomes the foundation for Theban independence and the subsequent rise of Thebes as a major power.
events.certainty_note: The existence of resistance is attested, though the specific details and chronology of the underground movement are less clearly documented than the occupation itself.

events.title: Athenian response to Spartan aggression
events.description: Athens responds to the Spartan occupation of Thebes with diplomatic protests and begins to consider military support for Theban resistance. The occupation of the Cadmea is widely seen as a violation of the King's Peace and provokes concern among other Greek city-states. Athens, while not yet openly supporting Theban liberation, begins to shift its diplomatic stance away from Sparta. This marks the beginning of Athens' alignment with anti-Spartan forces in Greece.
events.certainty_note: Athens' diplomatic response to the Cadmea occupation is attested, though the specific details and timing of individual diplomatic moves are less clearly documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The Spartan occupation of the Cadmea is one of the most clearly documented events of this period, and there is little disconfirming evidence regarding its occurrence or date. However, some sources suggest that Phoebidas' action may have been unauthorized or at least controversial within Sparta itself, and that Spartan leadership was divided on the wisdom of the occupation. The characterization of the occupation as a turning point is widely accepted, but some scholars argue that Spartan decline was already inevitable due to broader structural factors, and that the Cadmea occupation merely accelerated an existing trend.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 382 BCE is relatively rich, dominated by Xenophon's contemporary account in the Hellenica. This is one of the best-documented years of the early 4th century BCE. However, the sources are still selective and focus on political and military history. Economic and social history are underrepresented. The sources are heavily biased toward the Greek mainland and Aegean world; events in other regions are poorly documented or absent. Spartan sources are largely lost, and our knowledge of Spartan decision-making depends on Greek accounts, which are often hostile or incomplete.

graph_edges.note: Spartan occupation of Cadmea provokes Theban liberation movement
Cadmea occupation ultimately leads to Theban hegemony and Battle of Leuctra
